constraints: Streams 1 (unsupported gate) and 3 (policy removal) must land in the same release to prevent the inflated-score regression described in the trade-off analysis
The certifiableExts map in generic.go must NOT be modified — Swift files must still be discovered so the report banner can surface the count of unanalyzed units
CodeMetrics.Complexity pointer migration must be accompanied by nil-guards at every direct field access site (complexity.go:ComputeSymbolMetrics, complexity_test.go, metrics_test.go)

Score()'s violation loop must distinguish 'missing evidence' violations from threshold violations by matching on the Description prefix from evaluator.go:97, not by introducing a new Violation type (to keep scope minimal)
Existing on-disk swift.yml policy files from prior 'certify init' are not modified or deleted — only new inits are affected
The WeightedAverage function must continue to handle nil/empty dimension maps gracefully (returns 0)
All report formatters (JSON, text, YAML) must handle the new StatusUnsupported and GradeNA values
contractChanges: domain: new StatusUnsupported status, new GradeNA grade
evidence: ToEvidence() conditionally omits complexity key when nil
engine: Certify() / collectUnitEvidence() gate — unsupported → StatusUnsupported, GradeNA, skip scoring
engine: Score() skips dimension-introduction for violations with missing-evidence description
report: GenerateCard() excludes StatusUnsupported from aggregates; adds Unanalyzed count + banner
init: remove swiftStandardPolicy and case "swift" from languagePolicy()
openQuestions: Should the unsupported gate fire at discovery time (GenericScanner skips .swift entirely) or at evidence-collection time (unit is discovered but marked unsupported)? Discovery-time gating is cleaner but removes visibility — the report-card banner needs the count of unanalyzed units, which requires them to be discovered first
Should HasToolSupport be derived from a formal registry (like the analyzer Registry) or remain a hardcoded check in executor.go? A registry would future-proof but adds scope
What is the migration path for existing certification databases/JSON stores that contain Status='ok' records for Swift units? Re-certify? Backfill to StatusUnsupported?
Should the 'Insufficient Data' UI label appear in the report card summary row or only in the per-unit detail view?
Do we need a CLI flag (e.g. --include-unsupported) to force scoring of unsupported units for users who knowingly accept git-only metrics?
risks: Streams 1+3 must land together: if the unsupported gate lands without removing the SwiftLint policy template, existing Swift repos with stale swift.yml policies on disk may bypass the gate or produce confusing mixed signals

Pointer migration risk: changing Complexity from int to *int requires auditing all direct field accesses — complexity.go ComputeSymbolMetrics, complexity_test.go, metrics_test.go — to avoid nil-dereference panics
Report formatters that switch on Status without a default/catch-all case will render StatusUnsupported as a raw string or panic; all formatters (JSON, text, YAML) need the new string mapping

Existing persisted certification records in DB/JSON may have Status 'ok' for Swift units; a re-certify after the change will flip them to unsupported, which could surprise users comparing old vs new reports
The WeightedAverage change (fewer dimensions) could inflate scores for partially-analyzed languages if the gate is bypassed; Stream 4 must not land before Stream 1
summary: Introduce an "unsupported language" gate that intercepts units before scoring when no analyzer nor tool runner exists for the language; fix a data-quality bug where unmeasured complexity defaults to 0 and feeds scoring; remove the dead SwiftLint policy template; and reconcile the scoring contract so missing-evidence violations do not introduce phantom dimension scores.
